s_1.text:
The water is impossibly clear. Standing at the Adamant's rail, you can see the bottom — white sand rippled by current, dark patches of seagrass, and the coral heads rising twenty feet from the seabed like the pillars of a drowned church. Fish move through the shallows in quick silver pulses. The air is warm and still.

Pell stands beside you, stripped to his waist, a coil of rope over one shoulder. He has already tied a sounding weight to a measured line. Behind him, four of the crew wait with canvas bags and the iron grappling hooks you brought from Nassau.

You take out the chart — Morrow's chart, the one that brought you here. The notation is precise: a bearing, a depth, a small cross in red ink where the San Cristóbal went down. Beside it, in Morrow's tight handwriting: *42 ft. Listing to port. Main hold accessible at low water. Captain's cabin aft, upper deck — may be intact.*

Forty-two feet. Deep enough to kill a careless diver. Shallow enough to reach.

Pell reads the chart over your shoulder. "She's in the channel between those two big coral heads, Captain. See where the water turns dark? That's the drop-off. She'll be down there, on her side."

He looks at you steadily. Waiting for the order.

choices.text: Order the crew to prepare — they trust you
Order the crew to prepare for the dive

s_2.text:
The crew moves without hesitation. Whatever you built with these men in Nassau — the trust, the shared risk, the sense that this ship runs on something more than wages and fear — it holds.

Pell volunteers before you can ask. "I'll go first, Captain. Test the approach. If the hull's open where Morrow says it is, I'll mark the entry with a line and come back up."

Two other hands — Crewe, who can hold his breath longer than any man you've sailed with, and young Harker, who swam before he walked — strip down and begin preparing. They tie lines around their waists, check the canvas recovery bags, test the grappling hooks.

The rest of the crew takes positions along the rail, ready to manage the lines and haul up whatever comes from below.

Pell drops a sounding weight. The line runs out through his fingers — thirty, thirty-five, forty feet. It goes slack. "Bottom," he says. "Forty-one feet. Morrow was right."

He looks at you one more time, fills his lungs, and goes over the side.

choices.text: Watch from the rail as Pell descends

s_3.text:
You watch Pell's shape diminish as he pulls himself down the sounding line — a dark silhouette against white sand, shrinking with every stroke. At twenty feet the detail blurs. At thirty, he is a shadow. At forty, he reaches the bottom and the line goes still.

A minute passes. Then two. The crew watches in silence, hands on the safety lines.

Then the line jerks — three sharp tugs. Pell's signal: *I found it.*

He comes up slowly, hand over hand, and breaks the surface gasping. Water streams from his hair. He grabs the rail and pulls himself up enough to speak.

"She's there, Captain. The San Cristóbal. On her port side, like the chart says. The mainmast is gone — broke off and lying across the reef. But the hull..." He pauses, catching his breath. "The hull is open. Starboard gunport, midships. Big enough to swim through. I tied a guide line to the frame."

He pulls himself aboard and sits dripping on the deck. "The captain's cabin is aft, one deck up from the hold. There's a companionway — silted up, but passable. The cargo hold is forward of that, through the orlop. I could see crates."

He looks up at you. "What do you want first, Captain?"

choices.text: Go over the side — make the dive yourself
Send Pell back down for the captain's cabin
Send Pell back down for the cargo hold

s_5.text:
The crew does not move.

You give the order clearly — prepare to dive, four men in the water, lines and recovery bags — and for a long moment no one responds. They look at each other. They look at the water. They do not look at you.

Finally, Pell steps forward. Not eagerly. He has the decency to say it to your face, at least.

"Captain, the lads have concerns. Forty feet down into a wreck that's been on the bottom for ten years — that's not a swim, that's a burial detail. The San Cristóbal killed her own crew going down. And we don't know what's left of her. Could be the whole hull's rotten through. Could be she's full of silt and one wrong touch brings the deck down on top of you."

He pauses. "They'll do it. But they need to hear why it's worth the risk."

choices.text: Promise them a fair share of whatever you recover
Tell them this is bigger than gold — this is about Nassau's survival
Order them below — this is not a discussion

s_6.text:
"Every man who goes into the water gets a share," you say. "Not a captain's share — an equal cut. Whatever we bring up, it's split evenly among the dive crew."

That gets their attention. The mathematics of gold is a language every sailor speaks. Pell nods slowly. Crewe, the best swimmer among them, starts stripping down without being asked.

"Fair enough, Captain," Pell says. "We'll do it."

The mood shifts — still wary, but willing. They prepare the lines and bags with the careful attention of men who have just been given a reason to come back alive.

Pell drops a sounding weight. Forty-one feet. He fills his lungs and goes over the side.

choices.text: Watch as Pell descends to find the wreck

s_7.text:
"Listen to me," you say. "There is a man sitting in a sixty-gun warship at the mouth of Nassau harbour who killed two hundred of his own sailors and buried the evidence in that wreck. The Red Correspondence — that's what's down there. Letters with his name on them. Proof. The kind of proof that doesn't just end a career — it ends a blockade."

You let that settle.

"Ashford is not going away. Not with ships, not with negotiation, not with the Harbour Council writing letters to London. He goes away when someone puts those documents on a table in front of the right people. That's what this dive is for."

Pell looks at the crew. Something shifts in the group — not enthusiasm, but understanding. The dive isn't just dangerous. It's necessary.

"Right then," Pell says quietly. He starts stripping down. The others follow.

Pell drops the sounding weight. Forty-one feet. He fills his lungs and goes over the side.

choices.text: Watch as Pell descends to find the wreck

s_8.text:
"This is not a vote," you say. Your voice carries the full length of the deck. "You signed articles. You took your advance in Nassau. And I am telling you — every man who can swim is going into that water, and we are bringing up what is down there. That is an order."

Silence. The kind of silence that has weight to it.

Pell holds your gaze for a long moment, then nods — not in agreement, but in recognition. He has seen this before. A captain who has made a decision and will not be moved from it.

"You heard the Captain," he says to the crew, and begins stripping down.

They obey. But something has broken between you and these men. You can feel it in the way they handle the lines — competent but cold. Professional, not loyal. The difference matters.

choices.text: Watch as Pell descends to find the wreck

s_9.text:
Pell descends along the sounding line, his shape shrinking against the white sand below. At forty feet he reaches the bottom and the line goes still.

You wait. The crew waits. A minute. Then two.

Three sharp tugs on the line. He has found the wreck.

Pell surfaces gasping, water streaming from his face. "She's there, Captain. The San Cristóbal. Lying on her port side across the channel. The mainmast is gone but the hull is open — a gunport midships, wide enough to swim through. I tied a guide line."

He catches his breath. "Captain's cabin is aft, up one deck. Cargo hold forward through the orlop. Both look passable, but it's dark down there. Silt everywhere."

He waits for your decision.

test_luck.lucky_text: You feel the dive will go well — the water is calm, the visibility good, and Pell's report is encouraging. Luck is with you today.
test_luck.unlucky_text: As you prepare to follow Pell down, a wave of dread settles over you. The water looks darker than it did a moment ago. You push through it, but the dive will cost you.

s_10a.text:
The dive goes cleanly. You follow Pell's guide line down into the blue — pulling yourself hand over hand through water that gets colder with every foot of depth. At thirty feet the coral walls of the channel rise on either side, encrusted with fan coral and bright anemones. At forty feet the sand levels out and you see her.

The San Cristóbal.

She is enormous. Even lying on her side, half-buried in sand, she dominates the channel floor. Her stern is closest — you can read the name carved into the transom, the letters still sharp under a decade of marine growth: *SAN CRISTÓBAL — CÁDIZ*. A Crown crest above it, green with algae but legible.

Pell's guide line leads to the open gunport midships. You follow it, kicking steadily, and pull yourself through into the wreck.

The interior is dark. Shafts of green light fall through cracks in the hull above. Silt hangs in the water like fog. You can see the companionway leading aft and up — toward the captain's cabin. Forward, through a collapsed bulkhead, the orlop deck stretches into darkness. The cargo hold.

Your lungs are burning. You have time for one objective before you must surface.

choices.text: Take a moment to look around the wreck
Head aft to the captain's cabin — the Red Correspondence
Go forward into the cargo hold — the war chest

s_10b.text:
The dive is hard. You follow Pell's guide line down but the current pulls at you, and twice you lose your grip and have to kick back to the rope. The cold hits at twenty feet — a wall of it, stealing the warmth from your limbs.

At forty feet you reach the bottom and see her. The San Cristóbal, lying on her side across the channel, her stern facing you. The name is carved into the transom: *SAN CRISTÓBAL — CÁDIZ*. A Crown crest above, barely visible under the growth.

You pull yourself through the open gunport. Inside, the wreck is dark and silted. Your lungs are already burning. The companionway leads aft to the captain's cabin. The orlop stretches forward to the cargo hold.

You have time for one objective.

choices.text: Head aft to the captain's cabin — the Red Correspondence
Go forward into the cargo hold — the war chest

s_10.text:
The companionway is narrow and silted. You pull yourself up through it — the ship is on her side, so "up" means sideways, climbing along what was once a wall. Barnacles tear at your hands. The water here is darker, the light from the gunport fading behind you.

Then you reach the captain's cabin.

The door hangs open, swollen with water and age. Inside, the cabin is a ruin — furniture smashed against the port wall when the ship rolled, charts and papers turned to pulp, a brass telescope green with corrosion. A skeleton sits in the corner, held in place by a fallen beam across its chest. The captain of the San Cristóbal. Ten years in his chair.

But there — bolted to what was once the underside of the desk, now the wall — a brass tube. Sealed at both ends with wax and lead. Still intact. The Red Correspondence.

Your lungs are screaming. You wrench the tube free, tuck it into your belt, and kick for the companionway.

choices.text: Surface with the brass tube

s_11.text:
You break the surface gasping, one hand on the guide line and the other clutching the brass tube to your chest. Pell is at the rail — he grabs your arm and hauls you up until you can hook a leg over the gunwale.

You lie on the deck breathing. The sun is blinding after the green darkness below. The brass tube is heavy in your hand — heavier than it should be for its size, as if the weight of what it contains has a physical presence.

When you can sit up, you examine it. The seals are intact — lead caps pressed into red wax, stamped with a Crown cipher. The tube itself is green with age but sound. Whatever is inside has been kept dry for a decade on the bottom of the sea.

Pell crouches beside you. "Is that it, Captain? The Correspondence?"

The crew gathers. They are watching.

choices.text: Break the seals and read what is inside
Seal it away for now — there is still the cargo hold to recover

s_12.text:
You break the lead caps with the pommel of your cutlass. The wax cracks and falls away. Inside the tube: three sheets of heavy paper, tightly rolled, dry as the day they were written. You unroll them carefully.

The handwriting is precise and measured — an officer's hand, trained in correspondence. The signature at the bottom of each page is the same: *H. J. Ashford, Commodore, Jamaica Station.*

The first letter is dated June 14th, 1708. It is addressed to the Governor of Jamaica.

*"...the loss of the San Cristóbal is to be attributed to navigational failure and inclement weather. I have instructed the surviving officers to confirm this account. The prize money — forty-seven thousand pounds sterling — has been disbursed according to the attached schedule. The two hundred and fourteen souls lost in the action were predominantly Spanish prisoners and pressed hands of no particular consequence...*"

You read the line again. *Pressed hands of no particular consequence.*

Two hundred and fourteen men drowned so that Ashford could claim a prize that was already captured. He sank the San Cristóbal himself — scuttled her to cover the theft — and then wrote it up as an act of God.

Pell reads over your shoulder. His face goes still.

"That's the Commodore's hand," he says quietly. "I've seen it on enough warrants. That's him."

You roll the letters carefully and replace them in the tube.

choices.text: Prepare for the second dive — the cargo hold
The letters are enough — prepare to leave the Shallows

s_13.text:
You wrap the brass tube in oilcloth and stow it in your cabin. Whatever is inside can wait. The cargo hold is still down there — and with it, fifty thousand pounds in Crown gold.

Pell nods. "Smart. We don't know who's watching. Better to get everything up before we start reading."

The crew is already preparing for the second dive. The guide lines are still in place.

choices.text: Dive for the cargo hold
Wait until dawn — read the letters first, then dive

s_14.text:
The weight of what you have just read sits in your chest like a stone. Ashford killed those men. He killed them with paperwork and patience and a signature at the bottom of a letter, and then he spent ten years making sure no one found out.

But the gold is still down there. Fifty thousand pounds — enough to outfit a fleet, buy a pardon, or fund Nassau's defence for a year. The cargo hold is forward of where you found the cabin, deeper in the wreck.

Pell is already checking the guide lines. "The orlop is passable, Captain. I saw crates when I was down there. Big ones. Iron-bound."

The question is whether you have time.

choices.text: Dive for the war chest
The Correspondence is enough — prepare to leave

s_20.text:
The cargo hold is deeper in the wreck — forward, through the collapsed bulkhead on the orlop deck. You follow the guide line down again, the cold hitting you at twenty feet, the darkness closing in at thirty.

Inside the wreck, you pull yourself forward through the orlop. The space is tight — the ship's roll has compressed everything, beams and cargo and the remains of the lower gun deck all pressed together. You squeeze through a gap between two fallen frames and enter the hold.

It is here.

The Crown war chest sits against the hull, half-buried in silt. Iron-bound oak, the Crown seal still visible on the lid. It is enormous — three feet long, two feet deep, and heavier than anything one person can move. Through a crack in the lid you can see the dull gleam of coin. Gold. A king's ransom, sitting on the seabed.

But moving it will take multiple dives and at least three men. This is not a one-trip recovery.

choices.text: Begin the recovery — the crew is ready
Begin the recovery — rig lines and haul it up

s_21.text:
Your crew works like a machine. Pell organises the rigging while Crewe and Harker take turns diving — two men below at all times, threading rope under the chest, clearing the silt, widening the gap in the hull so the chest can be pulled through.

It takes four dives and the better part of two hours. The chest is too heavy to lift straight — they walk it across the seabed to the open gunport, rig a sling from the Adamant's boom, and haul it up through forty feet of water with the whole crew on the line.

It comes over the rail streaming seawater, landing on the deck with a thud that shakes the planking. The Crown seal stares up at you.

choices.text: Open the chest and see what is inside

s_22.text:
The crew works, but slowly. Every dive is a negotiation — who goes next, how long they stay, whether the lines are rigged safely enough. Without trust, every decision becomes a discussion. Without loyalty, every risk is weighed against personal survival.

Pell holds it together through sheer force of will, but it takes six dives and nearly three hours. Twice, men come up too fast and spend ten minutes bent over the rail, gasping. Once, a line slips and the chest drops back to the seabed, forcing a restart.

But eventually it comes over the rail — streaming seawater, landing on the deck with a thud. The Crown seal. Gold coins inside, dark with tarnish.

Fifty thousand pounds. Recovered at cost.

choices.text: Check the armoury before leaving the wreck
Secure the chest and prepare to leave the Shallows
There is still the captain's cabin to search

s_25.text:
There is one more space in the wreck you have not searched. The San Cristóbal's armoury — aft of the cargo hold, behind a reinforced bulkhead. When Pell was down, he saw the door: iron-strapped oak, still sealed.

You make one more dive. The armoury door takes three hard kicks to break open — the iron is corroded but the oak behind it is dense and waterlogged. Inside, the space is small. Racks of rusted muskets, useless. Cutlasses turned to orange lace. Shot lockers spilling corroded iron balls across the deck.

But in the back corner, in a lead-lined crate marked with a red diamond: four sealed canisters. Brass, heavy, each one the size of a wine bottle. The markings are military — Crown ordnance stamps, a batch number, and a warning etched into the metal: *CAUTION — COMPOUND VOLATILE — HANDLE WITH EXTREME CARE*.

Dragon's Breath. Ship-grade explosive compound. The San Cristóbal was carrying enough to blow a frigate in half.

Four canisters. Still sealed. Still volatile. Ten years on the seabed, and the brass has held.

Taking these is a risk. The compound inside could be stable. It could also detonate if dropped from any height. There is no way to know without opening a canister, and opening a canister underwater is not something you are prepared to do.

choices.text: Take the Dragon's Breath — the risk is worth it
Leave them — this is not worth dying for

s_26.text:
You wrap each canister individually in sailcloth and carry them up one at a time — four dives, each one careful and slow, each one with the knowledge that the thing in your hands could end you without warning.

The canisters sit on the Adamant's deck in a row, brass gleaming in the sun, water beading on the ordnance stamps. Pell looks at them the way you would look at a sleeping snake.

"Dragon's Breath," he says. "My old ship had a crate of those. Captain kept them locked in the magazine and only broke them out when we were fighting something too big to kill with chain shot." He pauses. "They work. God help me, they work. But if one of those goes off wrong, it takes the ship with it."

You stow them in the forward magazine, packed in sand, away from heat and friction.

choices.text: Prepare to leave the Shallows
There is still the captain's cabin to search

s_27.text:
You leave the canisters where they are. Some things are better left on the bottom of the sea. The San Cristóbal can keep her explosives.

You kick out through the gunport and surface for the last time.

choices.text: Prepare to leave the Shallows
There is still the captain's cabin to search

s_30.text:
The recovery is complete. The Adamant sits lower in the water than when she arrived — the weight of what you have brought up is measurable in the waterline.

The crew is exhausted. The diving has taken its toll — cold water, burning lungs, the strain of hauling heavy cargo through forty feet of ocean. Pell sits on a coil of rope, drying in the sun, a fresh cut across his forearm where the coral tore him on his last ascent.

The lagoon is still beautiful. The coral towers. The silver fish. The warm air. But the peace of it feels temporary now — like standing in a room where someone has just left and you can still smell the smoke.

You check the horizon. The reef channel — the one you came through — is the only way out of this lagoon. Beyond it, eight leagues of open water between here and Nassau.

Eight leagues. With what you are carrying, it might as well be eight hundred.

choices.text: A familiar sail — Morrow is already here
A sail on the horizon — and it is not friendly
A sail approaching the lagoon
A sloop entering the reef channel
Weigh anchor and make for the reef channel

s_31.text:
You do not need to check the horizon. You already know.

Morrow's sloop — the Ash Meridian — is anchored on the far side of the lagoon, tucked into the lee of the northern reef. She was here when you arrived. Her crew has been diving the wreck from the opposite approach — the bow section, where the San Cristóbal's chart room and navigation stores are located.

Morrow has not interfered with your recovery. Her divers stayed clear of the stern. Her crew watched your work from across the lagoon with the careful attention of professionals — noting what you recovered, how long it took, and what it cost you.

Now, as you prepare to leave, a boat puts out from the Ash Meridian. One person in it. Rowing steadily across the lagoon toward the Adamant.

Reyna Morrow. Alone.

choices.text: Wait for her at the rail

s_32.text:
She comes alongside and ties off without asking permission. Climbs the boarding ladder with the economy of someone who has done it ten thousand times. She stands on your deck in a salt-stained shirt, her dark hair tied back, a knife at her belt and nothing else.

She looks at the brass tube in your hand. The Red Correspondence. She looks at the war chest lashed to the deck. She does not ask what you found. She already knows.

"I read those letters three years ago," she says. "Before I sealed the tube and put it back. I needed someone else to bring it up. Someone who could do something with it that I could not."

She is quiet for a moment. The lagoon laps at the hull.

"Ashford killed my brother," she says. "Thomas Morrow. He was a pressed hand on the San Cristóbal. One of the two hundred and fourteen of no particular consequence." Her voice is level. Controlled. She has had three years to practice saying this without breaking. "I found the wreck looking for his grave. I found the letters instead."

She meets your eyes. "Take them to Nassau. Use them. End this. That is all I am asking."

She turns back to the ladder. The conversation is over. There is nothing else to say.

choices.text: Let her go — she has made her peace with this
Ask if she will sail with you back to Nassau

s_33.text:
A sail on the western horizon. Coming in through the reef channel — a sloop, fast and well-handled, threading the coral heads with the confidence of a captain who knows these waters.

The Ash Meridian. Morrow.

She anchors two hundred yards off your beam and puts a boat in the water. Not armed. One person rowing — Morrow herself — and a flag of parley at the bow.

The standoff holds. Her crew watches from the Meridian's rail. Your crew watches from yours. The lagoon is very quiet.

Morrow comes alongside. She does not board. She sits in her boat, one hand on the oar, looking up at you.

"I see you found what you were looking for, Captain," she says. Her eyes move to the brass tube, the war chest. She takes inventory without expression. "I am not here to contest it. But I want to know what you intend to do with what you found."

choices.text: Tell her about the Red Correspondence — what it contains
Tell her nothing — your business is your own

s_33a.text:
You hold up the brass tube. "Ashford's handwriting. Three letters, dated June 1708. He scuttled the San Cristóbal himself to cover a fraudulent prize claim. Two hundred and fourteen men died for it."

Morrow is quiet for a long time. The boat rocks gently. When she speaks, her voice is careful.

"I know," she says. "I knew before I gave you the chart. I needed someone to bring it to light who wasn't..."

She stops. Starts again.

"Just take it to Nassau. Use it right. That's all."

She pushes off from the Adamant's hull and rows back toward the Ash Meridian. Her crew hauls her aboard. Within the hour, the Meridian's anchor comes up and she makes for the reef channel. She does not look back.

choices.text: Prepare to leave the Shallows

s_33b.text:
"You gave me the chart, Morrow. That was your choice. What I do with what I found is mine."

She looks at you. Something passes across her face — not anger, not surprise. Something closer to recognition. She has dealt with captains before. She knows what she is looking at.

"Fair enough," she says. She pushes off and rows back to the Ash Meridian.

Within the hour, the Meridian weighs anchor and makes for the channel. She sails west. You will not see her again until Nassau.

choices.text: Prepare to leave the Shallows

s_34.text:
Morrow climbs down the ladder, unties her boat, and rows back across the lagoon. You watch her go. The Ash Meridian's crew hauls her aboard.

Twenty minutes later, the Meridian weighs anchor and slips out through the reef channel. Heading north. Back to Nassau, ahead of you. Whatever she does next is her own business.

The lagoon is quiet again. Your crew is watching you.

Pell clears his throat. "Orders, Captain?"

choices.text: Weigh anchor and make for the reef channel
Wait until morning — the crew needs rest

s_34b.text:
"Morrow. Sail with us. Two ships are harder to intercept than one, and if Ashford's patrol is out there—"

"No." The word is flat. Final. "The Ash Meridian sails alone. I have my own course to Nassau, and my own business when I arrive."

She climbs down the ladder. Pauses. "You will be fine, Captain. You have what you need."

She rows back across the lagoon. The Ash Meridian weighs anchor and departs through the reef channel, heading north.

The lagoon is quiet. Pell is waiting for orders.

choices.text: Weigh anchor and make for the reef channel

s_35.text:
Movement at the reef channel. A sail — coming in fast, threading the coral with aggressive confidence. Then a second sail behind the first.

The Ash Meridian. And a captured fishing smack, crewed by Morrow's men and armed with swivel guns.

Morrow is not here to talk.

Her sloop drives straight for the Adamant's beam, cutting off the channel exit. The fishing smack swings wide to cover the northern passage. You are penned in.

A voice carries across the water — Morrow's first mate, standing at the Meridian's bow with a speaking trumpet.

"Captain Morrow's compliments. She'll have the cargo, or she'll have a fight. Your choice."

choices.text: Fight — clear the channel by force
Negotiate — offer to share the prize
Use what you know about Morrow's secret
Flee — cut your cable and run for the northern passage

s_36.text:
You give the order. The Adamant's guns run out — four six-pounders per side, loaded with chain shot. Not enough to sink the Meridian, but enough to cripple her rigging and force her to give way.

The first broadside tears across the lagoon. Morrow's crew fires back — her swivel guns bark, and musket balls crack against the Adamant's hull. A furious, close-range exchange in water too shallow for manoeuvre.

Morrow's enforcer leads a boarding party — three men in a boat, coming alongside with grappling hooks and cutlasses. You meet them at the rail.

s_36a.text:
The enforcer goes down hard, his cutlass clattering on the deck. The other two boarders see him fall and drop back into their boat, pulling for the Meridian.

Morrow watches from her quarterdeck. She is not a fool — she can count casualties and calculate odds. The Adamant is wounded but fighting. The Meridian has taken a broadside through her foretopsail. The mathematics have shifted.

A long moment. Then the Meridian's guns go silent. Her crew begins cutting away the damaged rigging. Morrow turns her sloop toward the channel and makes way.

She will remember this. But for now, the passage is clear.

choices.text: Make for the reef channel before she changes her mind

s_37.text:
You lean over the rail and call across the water. "Morrow! A word, if you are not in too much of a hurry to listen."

A pause. Then Morrow herself appears at the Meridian's rail. She does not have a speaking trumpet. She does not need one — her voice carries like a bosun's.

"Talk fast, Captain."

"Split the gold. Half for you, half for me. The Correspondence stays with me — it is worth nothing to you as coin, and everything to Nassau as leverage. You get twenty-five thousand pounds. I get the letters. We both sail away."

Morrow considers. The lagoon is quiet except for the creak of rigging and the lap of water.

"Twenty-five thousand," she says. "And I never see those letters. They go straight to the Council. Not to Ashford. Not to anyone who might use them for a private arrangement."

"Agreed."

The exchange takes an hour. Half the coins from the war chest transferred to the Meridian in canvas bags. Morrow's men count every piece. When it is done, the Meridian pulls back from the channel and Morrow nods from her quarterdeck.

The passage is clear.

choices.text: Make for the reef channel

s_38.text:
You call across the water. "Morrow. I know about Thomas."

The silence that follows is absolute. Even the water seems to stop moving.

Morrow stands at the Meridian's rail. Her face does not change. But her hands, gripping the wood, go white at the knuckles.

"I know why you had the chart," you continue. "I know what was on the San Cristóbal that mattered to you. It was never the gold. It was never the letters. It was a manifest with your brother's name on it."

Morrow does not speak for a long time. When she does, her voice is different — stripped down, the authority gone, something raw underneath.

"Pull your guns in," she says. "I am leaving."

The Meridian withdraws. Morrow does not look at you again. The fishing smack follows her out through the channel. The lagoon empties.

You have the passage. But something about the way she left — the angle of her shoulders, the silence of her crew — tells you that this is not over. It is merely postponed.

choices.text: Make for the reef channel

s_39.text:
You give the order to cut the anchor cable. The Adamant lurches free, catching the wind, and drives for the northern passage — a narrow gap between two coral heads that Morrow's fishing smack is trying to block.

The smack fires its swivel guns. Shot punches through the Adamant's sail — holes appear in the canvas, rigging parts with a crack. But the Adamant has momentum. You drive straight through the gap, the coral scraping along the hull with a sound like tearing cloth, and burst out into open water on the far side.

Behind you, Morrow's sloop is turning to pursue, but she has to thread back through the channel first. You have a lead.

choices.text: Run for the open sea

s_40.text:
The reef channel. The way you came in — a narrow cut through the coral, barely wide enough for the Adamant's beam, with the reef rising on either side like broken teeth. The water here changes colour — from the clear turquoise of the lagoon to the deep Atlantic blue beyond.

The current is stronger on the outward passage. The tide is turning, pulling water out through the channel, and the Adamant rides it like a horse finding its stride.

Pell stands at the bow, watching the water. The coral heads slide past — close enough to touch, close enough to hear the water breaking over them.

choices.text: Let Sable take the helm through the reef
Take the helm yourself

s_41.text:
Sable takes the helm.

She does not ask. She moves to the wheel with the quiet certainty of someone who knows that this is her moment — the reason you brought her, the thing she can do that no one else on this ship can do.

Her eyes read the water the way a musician reads a score. She sees things you cannot — the subtle colour shift that marks a submerged ledge, the ripple pattern that reveals the current's true direction, the place where the coral drops away and the channel deepens by three critical feet.

"Helm to port," she says. "Now starboard. Hold... hold... now hard to port."

The Adamant threads the channel like a needle through cloth. The coral slides past — inches from the hull, close enough that Pell flinches — but Sable's hands are steady and her corrections are precise. She takes you through on a single tack, using the outgoing tide to carry you past the worst of the heads.

The Atlantic opens ahead. Deep water. The reef falls away behind.

Sable steps back from the wheel. She does not smile. She does not need to.

"Clean passage, Captain," she says.

choices.text: Take one last look at the Shallows
Set course for Nassau

s_42.text:
Without a pilot, you take the helm yourself. The chart shows the channel, but the chart is Morrow's work and you do not know how accurate it is at this scale. The coral heads do not care about your chart.

You call out marks to Pell at the bow. He watches the water, looking for the dark shapes beneath the surface — the coral that could open the Adamant's hull like a knife through canvas.

test_luck.lucky_text:
The channel holds. You find the deepest water by instinct or grace — the Adamant slides through with her keel clearing the coral by less than a foot. Pell calls out a warning at the last bend, you throw the helm over, and the sloop answers. Clear water opens ahead.

"That was close, Captain," Pell says. His voice is not entirely steady.
test_luck.unlucky_text:
The hull strikes coral. Not hard — a glancing blow along the starboard quarter — but the sound is terrible. A grinding, cracking noise that vibrates through the whole ship. Water begins seeping through the planking below the waterline.

Pell rushes below and comes back with a grim report. "She is taking water, Captain. Not fast — the pumps will hold it — but the hull is damaged. We need to get to Nassau before it gets worse."

s_43.text:
The Adamant clears the channel, but she is wounded. The hull damage is below the waterline — a long scrape where the coral tore through the copper sheathing and into the planking. The pumps are working, but the crew is already tired from the diving and now they have to man the bilge handles as well.

Pell organises the pump rotation — two men at a time, twenty minutes on, twenty off. It is sustainable, but it costs energy you cannot afford.

The reef falls away behind. Open water ahead. Nassau is eight leagues to the north.

choices.text: Press on — the crew will hold together
Press on for Nassau

s_44.text:
The crew holds. They are tired, they are cold, and the pumps never stop — but they hold. Pell keeps the rotation moving. Crewe takes double shifts without being asked. Even young Harker, who has been grey-faced since the coral strike, works his turns at the bilge handles without complaint.

The Adamant limps north. She is slower than she should be — the hull damage creates drag, and the water in the bilge shifts her balance. But she moves. She answers the helm. She carries you.

Eight leagues. The longest eight leagues of your life.

choices.text: Continue north toward Nassau

s_44a.text:
The crew is breaking.

The pump rotation falls apart within the first hour. Men refuse their turns. Arguments erupt at the bilge handles — not about the work, but about everything that has gone wrong since you ordered them into the water without asking. The coercion you used to start the dive has curdled into something worse. They do not trust you, and a crew that does not trust its captain does not work.

Pell holds the ship together by force of will, but even he cannot be everywhere. The pumps stall twice. The water in the bilge rises.

test_luck.lucky_text:
By some miracle of current and wind, the Adamant holds together. The hull damage does not worsen. Pell bullies the pump crew into one final sustained effort, and the bilge level drops enough to stabilize the ship.

You will make Nassau. Barely.
test_luck.unlucky_text:
The hull gives way. The coral damage opens under the pressure of the sea, and the Atlantic pours in through the starboard quarter. The pumps cannot keep up. The Adamant begins to settle.

Pell sees it first. "She is going down, Captain."

The crew abandons the pumps and begins cutting the ship's boat free. There is no panic — they are too exhausted for panic. They move with the mechanical efficiency of men who have accepted what is happening.

death_shallows.text: The sea took her ten years ago. Today it takes you. The shallows hold the light differently down here — green and gold and very still. The Adamant is somewhere above the surface. Your crew will make for Nassau. Someone else will find the Red Correspondence eventually. You just won't be there to see it.

s_45.text:
Open water. The Atlantic stretches north toward the Bahama Banks, deep blue and restless. The Adamant moves well — or well enough, given what she has been through. The wind is from the southeast, which means a broad reach to Nassau. Good sailing, if nothing else goes wrong.

The afternoon light is long and golden. The wreck is behind you now — the San Cristóbal, the coral, the green darkness of the dive. What you carry is all that remains of it. A brass tube. A chest of gold. Maybe four canisters of something that could end a ship.

Pell brings you coffee. Ship's coffee — thick and bitter and barely drinkable. He stands beside you at the helm and looks north.

"How far to Nassau, Captain?"

"Six leagues. Maybe five, with this wind."

"And what's between us and the harbour?"

That is the question.

choices.text: Feel the weight of what you carry
Scan the horizon — Ashford's arrangement should hold
Scan the horizon for Crown patrols

s_46.text:
One ship. A single Crown frigate — the Fervent, by the cut of her jib — sitting two leagues north of the Shallows on a lazy patrol. She has seen you. She must have. The Adamant's sail is visible for miles in this light.

But she does not close.

Ashford's arrangement. The back-channel you opened in Nassau — the understanding, spoken or unspoken, that you would not be interfered with as long as certain conditions were met. The Fervent is watching, not intercepting. Her captain has orders, and those orders say: let this one pass.

As you draw closer, a signal breaks from the frigate's mast. Flag signals — not a hail or a challenge, but a private signal from her flag lieutenant. You read the flags through your glass.

choices.text: Signal back — acknowledge the arrangement
Ignore the signal — keep sailing

s_47.text:
You order the Adamant's signal halyard run up — a simple acknowledgment, nothing that could be read by anyone else as anything other than a merchant's courtesy. The Fervent's flag lieutenant sees it and the frigate adjusts course slightly, opening the passage to Nassau by another quarter mile.

The arrangement holds.

Pell watches the exchange with a sailor's eye. He does not ask what it means. He is smart enough to know that some things are better left unasked. But he sees the frigate pull away, and he sees the clear water ahead, and he draws his own conclusions.

"Smooth sailing, Captain," he says. There is a question in it that he does not quite ask.

choices.text: Continue to Nassau

s_48.text:
You sail past the Fervent without acknowledgment. The frigate watches you go — a hundred guns and four hundred men, sitting idle as a sloop loaded with Crown gold and Crown secrets passes within a league of her broadside.

The flag lieutenant's signal hangs unanswered. After ten minutes, the Fervent hauls it down.

Whatever arrangement you made with Ashford, you have not rejected it. You have simply declined to confirm it. The ambiguity is deliberate. When you arrive in Nassau, you want to be able to say, truthfully, that you exchanged nothing with Crown forces on the open water.

Pell watches the frigate shrink behind you. "They let us go, Captain."

"They did."

"That normal?"

"No, Pell. It is not."

choices.text: Continue to Nassau

s_50.text:
Two ships.

They appear on the northern horizon as the afternoon shadows lengthen — two Crown vessels running south in line, their sails catching the late sun. The Fervent and the Resolve. Ashford has sent his patrol wide, and they have found you.

The lead ship — the Fervent — alters course to intercept. She is faster than the Adamant, and she has the wind gauge. At her current rate, she will be within cannon range in twenty minutes.

Pell sees them first. "Crown ships, Captain. Two of them. Bearing north-northeast, closing fast."

The crew freezes. Every man on deck is looking at you.

choices.text: Run — crowd on every sail and try to outpace them
Fight — bring the guns to bear
Bluff — fly merchant colours and try to talk your way through

s_51.text:
"All hands! Every stitch of canvas — topsails, staysails, the flying jib. Everything she'll carry!"

The crew scrambles into the rigging. The Adamant is a sloop — light, fast, built for exactly this. Her sails fill and she heels over, bow cutting through the Atlantic swells, spray breaking over the foredeck.

The Fervent is fast. But the Adamant is faster in this wind — a beam reach with the afternoon sea breeze filling in. The gap opens. Slowly at first, then with increasing certainty.

The Fervent fires a ranging shot — a single cannon ball that raises a waterspout a hundred yards astern. A warning. Come to, or we will fire for effect.

You do not come to.

test_luck.lucky_text:
The wind holds. The Adamant pulls away, knot by knot, until the Crown ships are hull-down on the horizon. They do not have the speed to catch you in this breeze, and by the time the wind shifts, you will be in the approaches to Nassau.

Pell lets out a breath he has been holding for ten minutes. "We are clear, Captain."
test_luck.unlucky_text:
The wind shifts. The sea breeze dies as the evening approaches, and the Adamant's speed drops. The Fervent closes the gap — her greater sail area tells in the lighter airs.

A broadside crashes out. Chain shot tears through the Adamant's rigging — the fore staysail parts, the main halyard is severed, and the boom swings wild. The Adamant staggers.

You cannot outrun them now. But you can still fight.

s_52.text:
The Adamant turns to fight. Her four six-pounders run out — small guns against a frigate's broadside, but in close quarters, at this range, they can hurt.

The Fervent closes. Her bow chasers fire — shot whistles overhead, punching through the main topsail. The Adamant answers with her starboard battery, and the exchange begins.

It is not a battle. It is a brawl. Two ships hammering each other at close range, the smoke rolling across the water, the sound of cannon and splintering wood filling the air.

A boarding party launches from the Fervent — marines, armed and disciplined. Their officer leads them over the Adamant's rail with a pistol in one hand and a sword in the other.

s_52a.text:
The naval officer falls. His marines hesitate — without their leader, the boarding action stalls. Pell and the crew push them back to the rail, and the marines retreat to their boat.

The Fervent pulls back to reassess. The Resolve is still closing, but she is ten minutes behind — ten minutes you can use.

The Adamant is battered. Her rigging is damaged, her hull is holed above the waterline in two places, and the deck is slick with blood. But she floats. She sails. And the channel to Nassau is open.

You take it.

choices.text: Run for Nassau while they regroup

s_53.text:
You haul down the Adamant's colours and run up a merchant ensign — the flag of a trading vessel out of Charleston, neutral and uninteresting. You order the guns housed, the war chest covered with canvas, and the crew to stand easy. Look like a merchant. Act like a merchant. Be a merchant.

The Fervent closes. Her captain examines you through his glass — a long, uncomfortable inspection. You can see the marines on her deck, ready. The gun ports are open.

A hail from the Fervent's quarterdeck: "What vessel? State your business!"

test_luck.lucky_text:
"Trading sloop Patience, out of Charleston," you call back. "Bound for Nassau with dry goods and timber. We were sheltering from weather in the lagoon."

The Fervent's captain considers. He looks at the Adamant — her lines are right for a merchant, and you are flying the right flag. The war chest is hidden. The crew looks civilian enough.

"Carry on," he calls. The Fervent alters course and resumes her patrol.

Pell does not relax until the frigate is a mile away. "That," he says, "was the most convincing lie I have ever witnessed."
test_luck.unlucky_text:
"Trading sloop Patience, out of Charleston," you call back. But the Fervent's captain is not convinced. He sees something — maybe the gun ports, imperfectly concealed. Maybe the brass tube you are holding. Maybe just the instinct of a man who has spent twenty years stopping ships.

"Heave to for inspection," he calls. Marines line the rail, muskets ready.

You have seconds to decide.

s_54.text:
You break away from the boarding fight, leaving Pell to hold the rail. The Adamant's helm is free — you throw it over, and the sloop swings away from the Fervent, the gap between the two ships opening as the wind fills the damaged sails.

The marines do not follow. They have lost their officer and their momentum. The Fervent fires a parting broadside — most of it goes high, tearing through rigging that is already damaged. One ball strikes the hull below the waterline. The Adamant shudders.

But you are moving. The channel is open. Nassau is ahead.

The damage is real, though. The hull is taking water, the rigging is shredded, and the crew is battered. You will make Nassau. But the Adamant may not survive another fight.

choices.text: Limp toward Nassau

s_55.text:
The last five leagues.

The Atlantic is quiet now. The afternoon is giving way to evening, and the light has turned gold — the particular gold of the Caribbean at the end of the day, when the sea and the sky are the same colour and the horizon disappears.

The Adamant sails north. She is carrying more weight than she was built for — the war chest alone shifted her waterline by two inches, and whatever else you recovered from the San Cristóbal adds to the burden. She moves through the water with the heavy, deliberate motion of a ship that is working harder than she should.

Pell sits on the foredeck, cleaning the cut on his arm with seawater and a strip of linen. The crew is quiet. The excitement of the dive and the tension of the patrol have drained out of them, leaving something closer to exhaustion.

You stand at the helm and watch the horizon. Somewhere ahead, invisible in the haze, Nassau is waiting. The blockade. The harbour. The Harbour Council. The questions that will be asked about what you found and what you intend to do with it.

The brass tube sits in your cabin. The letters inside it are ten years old, but they are as dangerous as anything the San Cristóbal ever carried. More dangerous than the gold. More dangerous than the Dragon's Breath.

Ashford's signature. Ashford's crime. Ashford's reckoning.

You adjust the helm and hold your course.

choices.text: Watch for Nassau on the horizon

s_56.text:
The first sign of Nassau is the smoke. Then the fort on its hill — a dark shape against the evening sky, the Union Jack barely visible in the failing light. Then the harbour, opening out below the town like a hand held out in greeting.

And the blockade.

Four ships. Crown's Justice sitting broadside at the harbour mouth, her sixty guns run out for the evening watch. Fervent and Resolve on the flanks. And the fourth — a new arrival, a corvette you do not recognise, sitting close inshore.

The blockade is tighter than when you left.

Pell joins you at the rail. "That corvette was not there when we sailed, Captain."

"No. It was not."

"Ashford's reinforcements?"

"Or someone else's."

The Adamant drifts. Nassau is two miles away. The harbour is right there — you can see the torches on the dock, the lights in the tavern windows, the shapes of ships at anchor. Everything you have been working toward is on the other side of that line of warships.

choices.text: Run the blockade — straight through, under cover of darkness
Wait for full dark — slip in along the coast

s_57.text:
No waiting. The Adamant drives for the harbour mouth as the last of the daylight dies. You run dark — no lanterns, no signals, every light aboard snuffed. The sloop is a shadow on black water, and you are betting everything on the fact that a shadow is harder to hit than a ship.

The Crown's Justice sees you. A challenge gun fires — a single report that rolls across the harbour. Then a second. The Fervent's bow chasers open up, firing blind into the darkness.

Shot falls around the Adamant — waterspouts erupting in the black water, the sound of cannon and the whine of iron. One ball strikes the stern rail and shatters it. Splinters fly across the quarterdeck.

But you are through. The Adamant slips between the Crown's Justice and the shore batteries, into the harbour, into safety. The guns fall silent. They cannot fire into the anchorage without hitting the town.

choices.text: Bring the Adamant alongside the dock

s_58.text:
You wait. The Adamant drifts two miles offshore, sails furled, riding the current in silence. The crew sleeps — or tries to. The watch changes. The stars come out, hard and bright.

At two bells in the middle watch — one o'clock in the morning — you give the order. The sails go up in darkness, the crew working by touch and habit. The Adamant moves inshore, hugging the coastline, using the land to mask her silhouette against the star field.

The blockade ships are black shapes against the harbour lights. You can see their riding lights — steady orange points that mark their positions. The gap between Crown's Justice and the shore is narrow, but it is there.

Pell navigates by the shore lights. He knows this approach — every rock, every shallow, every sandbar. He has sailed in and out of Nassau in darkness before, in worse conditions, with more at stake.

"Steady as she goes, Captain," he whispers. "Two hundred yards to the channel. One hundred. Fifty..."

The Adamant glides past the Crown's Justice close enough to hear voices on her deck. A sentry coughs. A lantern swings. But the darkness holds, and the sloop passes unseen.

choices.text: Enter the harbour

s_60.text:
Nassau harbour at night. The water is black and still, reflecting the dock lights in long rippled streaks. The Adamant comes alongside the main wharf — ropes fly, hands catch them, and the sloop is made fast.

You step ashore. The dock is quiet — a few drunks, a harbour watchman, a dog sleeping under an overturned boat. The town is dark except for the taverns on the waterfront, where lamplight spills through shuttered windows and you can hear the low murmur of men who have been drinking since sunset.

Pell supervises the unloading. The war chest — if you recovered it — comes off under guard, carried by four men to the harbourmaster's warehouse. The brass tube goes inside your coat, against your chest. You can feel the weight of it with every step.

The Adamant rests at the dock. Whatever she has been through today — the dive, the coral, the patrol, the blockade — she has survived it. She will need repairs. New copper on the hull. Fresh rigging. Time and money you may not have.

But that is tomorrow's problem.

choices.text: Head for the Harbour Council to report
Find a room at the tavern — report in the morning
Stay aboard the Adamant — sleep on the ship

s_61.text:
The Harbour Council meets in the back room of the harbourmaster's office — a low-ceilinged space that smells of tar and tobacco, lit by whale-oil lamps that make everything look older than it is.

At this hour, only two members are present: Whitmore, the harbourmaster himself, and Adjeke, who handles the Council's finances. They look up from a table covered in papers and ledgers.

Whitmore sees the brass tube first. His eyes sharpen.

"You found it," he says. Not a question.

"I found it. And more."

You lay it out for them — the dive, the wreck, the Red Correspondence, the war chest. The letters you read in the captain's cabin of a dead ship. Ashford's name. Ashford's crime.

Whitmore reads the first letter in silence. When he finishes, he sets it down on the table and sits back.

"This changes everything," he says. "This changes the entire arithmetic of the blockade."

Adjeke nods. She is already thinking about what comes next — the politics, the leverage, the careful application of pressure to the right points.

"Captain," she says. "You should rest. What happens next will require you at your best. And what happens next begins at dawn."

choices.text: Accept the advice — you have earned a night's rest

s_62.text:
The Broken Wheel tavern is warm and dark and smells of rum and woodsmoke. The barman — a large man with a missing ear and an expression of permanent disinterest — pours you a drink without being asked. He has seen captains come in from the sea before. He knows the look.

You sit in a corner booth with your back to the wall and the brass tube on the table in front of you. The rum is rough and burns going down. You drink it anyway.

The tavern is quiet. A few sailors. A card game in the back. The kind of night where nothing happens and everything is about to.

Pell finds you an hour later. He has secured the ship and posted a watch. He sits down across from you and looks at the brass tube.

"You should sleep, Captain. Tomorrow is going to be a long day."

He is right. Whatever the Red Correspondence means — whatever it changes — it will not change tonight. Tonight you are a captain who has just dived a wreck and run a blockade. Tonight you are tired.

You finish your rum and go upstairs.

choices.text: Sleep — tomorrow, everything changes

s_65.text:
You sleep. You dream of green water and gold light and the name carved into a dead ship's stern. When you wake, the sun is up and Nassau is already moving — the sound of hammers from the shipyard, the cry of gulls, the distant rumble of the harbour coming alive.

The brass tube is where you left it. The Red Correspondence. Ashford's confession, written in his own hand, sealed for a decade on the bottom of the sea.

Pell is waiting on the dock when you come out. He has coffee — real coffee, not the ship's tar — and he hands it to you without ceremony.

"Council wants to see you, Captain. Word has gotten around that the Adamant came in loaded last night. People are asking questions."

He pauses.

"And there are Crown marines in town. A landing party from the corvette. They came ashore at first light. Four of them, armed, with a flag of truce."

He looks at you steadily.

"Ashford wants to talk."

choices.text: Go to the Council first — present the Correspondence
Meet Ashford's marines first — hear what he wants
Walk through town first — take the measure of things

s_66.text:
The Council is assembled in full — Whitmore, Adjeke, and three others you have seen but never spoken to. They sit around the long table, and the brass tube sits in the center of it like an unexploded shell.

You tell them what you found. The dive. The wreck. The letters. You read the key passage aloud — Ashford's words, Ashford's handwriting, Ashford's admission that he scuttled a ship with two hundred and fourteen souls aboard to cover a fraudulent prize claim.

The room is silent when you finish.

Whitmore speaks first. "This is the lever we have been looking for. Ashford cannot maintain the blockade if this reaches London. His career, his commission, his freedom — all of it ends the moment these letters are presented to the Admiralty."

Adjeke leans forward. "The question is how we use it. Publicly — send it to London and wait for justice — or privately. Confront Ashford directly. Force him to lift the blockade as the price of our silence."

The Council looks at you. The decision, it seems, is yours.

But that decision can wait. There is a reckoning coming — with Ashford, with the blockade, with the future of Nassau itself. And it begins now.

choices.text: Prepare for what comes next

s_67.text:
The Crown marines wait at the harbourmaster's office — four men in red coats, muskets grounded, standing in the precise formation of soldiers who are very far from home and very aware of it. Their officer is a young lieutenant with a sunburned face and the careful expression of a man delivering a message he does not fully understand.

"Captain," he says. "Commodore Ashford presents his compliments and requests a meeting at your earliest convenience. The matter is one of mutual interest."

He hands you a sealed letter. The seal is Ashford's — the same seal you saw on the Red Correspondence, pressed in red wax.

You break it open. Inside, three lines in Ashford's hand:

*Captain — I know what you recovered from the San Cristóbal. I know what those letters contain. I propose a conversation. The alternative is one neither of us will enjoy. — H.J.A.*

Pell reads it over your shoulder. "He's scared, Captain. A commodore does not write like that unless he's scared."

The lieutenant waits for your response.

But that response — whatever it is — belongs to the next chapter. The Shallows are behind you. Nassau is around you. And ahead, the full weight of what you are carrying is about to come down.

You pocket the letter and turn toward the town.

choices.text: The reckoning begins

s_70.text:
The morning after the dive. You sit on the Adamant's stern rail, watching the harbour. The ship is quiet — most of the crew is ashore, spending their advance in the taverns and chandleries of the waterfront.

Pell sits on the deck below, splicing a new main halyard. His hands work automatically — tuck, pull, tuck — while his eyes stay on the harbour mouth. The blockade ships are visible in the morning light, black shapes against the glitter of the open sea.

"You know what I keep thinking about, Captain?" he says, not looking up from his work. "That skeleton in the cabin. The captain of the San Cristóbal. Ten years sitting at his desk with a beam across his chest. Ten years, and no one came for him."

He pulls a tuck tight.

"Ashford knew he was down there. Ashford put him down there. And he just... left."

The halyard takes shape under his hands. He does not say anything else. He does not need to. The silence says it for him — the particular silence of a man who has seen something that has changed the way he thinks about the world.

You watch the harbour and drink your coffee and let the morning happen.

choices.text: Head into town — there is work to do

s_71.text:
Below the waterline, the San Cristóbal is a cathedral. Her ribs arc overhead — massive oak frames, dark with age, hung with curtains of algae that move in the current like slow green flags. Shafts of light fall through the cracks in her hull, cutting through the murk in solid bars of green and gold.

You swim through her bones. The water is cold here, away from the sun, and your breath comes in tight bursts. Everything is slow — the current, the light, the way the silt billows up from the deck when your fins brush it.

A crab the size of your fist watches you from a cannon ball. A moray eel withdraws into a gun port as you pass. The San Cristóbal is alive — not with her crew, but with the reef. Ten years on the bottom, and the ocean has remade her in its own image.

The name is still legible on the stern transom, seen from inside now — reversed, the letters shadowed in marine growth. *LABÓTSIRG NAS*. The wrong way around, like everything else down here.

choices.text: Continue exploring the wreck

s_72.text:
The war chest is open.

Pell pries the lid with a marlinspike while the crew watches. The iron hinges resist, then give way with a screech that echoes across the lagoon. Inside, packed tight in oilcloth and lead foil: gold coins. Spanish doubloons, English guineas, Portuguese moidores — a mixed treasury, stamped with the faces of three kings, all of them dead.

The crew stares. Most of these men have never seen this much gold in one place. Some of them have never held a gold coin. The silence is the silence of men confronting something they have only ever imagined.

Pell picks up a single doubloon. Turns it in his fingers. The face of Philip V looks back at him — serene, indifferent, minted for a purpose the king never imagined.

"Fifty thousand pounds," Pell says quietly. "Give or take."

He puts the coin back. Closes the lid. Looks at you.

"What's the plan for it, Captain?"

choices.text: Check the armoury before leaving the wreck
Secure the chest and prepare to leave the Shallows
There is still the captain's cabin to search

s_73.text:
The descent.

You hold the guide line and pull yourself down. The first ten feet are warm — the sun heats the surface, and the water here is clear and bright, full of colour. Schools of sergeant majors flash past in yellow and black stripes. A parrotfish, absurdly blue, watches you descend with one unblinking eye.

At twenty feet the temperature drops. The warm layer ends and the cold begins — a wall of it, sudden and complete. Your skin contracts. Your lungs tighten.

At thirty feet the light changes. The greens deepen. The coral walls of the channel rise on either side — massive structures, centuries old, encrusted with fan coral and fire coral and brain coral in shapes that look designed rather than grown. Sea fans wave in the current, purple and orange, impossibly delicate.

At forty feet you reach the bottom. White sand, hard-packed, rippled by the current. And there, filling the channel from wall to wall, lying on her port side with her keel exposed to the sky:

The San Cristóbal.

She is magnificent. And she is terrible. A warship turned into a reef, her hull colonized by a decade of growth, her guns pointing at nothing, her name still carved into her stern as if she is still waiting for someone to read it.

choices.text: Approach the wreck

s_74.text:
The evening before the return. The crew builds a fire on the beach — driftwood and coconut husks, crackling in the warm night air. The lagoon reflects the stars.

Pell produces a bottle of rum from somewhere — he will not say where — and passes it around. The crew drinks. The conversation is quiet at first, then louder. Stories. The dive. Who went deepest. Who stayed longest. Who saw the skeleton in the captain's cabin and who pretended they did not.

You sit apart, on a rock at the water's edge, and listen. The fire paints the crew in orange light. These are the men who went into the dark with you — who swam through a dead ship forty feet below the surface and brought back what was inside. Whatever they are — pirates, sailors, men without better options — tonight they are yours.

Pell sits down beside you. He does not offer the bottle. He knows you are not drinking.

"Early start tomorrow, Captain?"

"First light."

"Aye." He looks at the fire. "It was a good dive. Whatever happens next — it was a good dive."

He goes back to the crew. You sit on the rock and watch the stars and listen to the lagoon.

choices.text: Dawn — check the reef before departing

s_75.text:
The weight of the war chest changes everything.

You can feel it in the way the Adamant sits — lower in the water, heavier on the helm. The sloop that danced through the reef channel on the way in now moves with deliberation. Every wave she takes is a reminder of what she is carrying.

Pell notices it too. "She is sluggish, Captain. Two knots slower than she should be. That chest must weigh four hundredweight."

Four hundredweight of gold. The Jamaica garrison's payroll, sitting in your hold, dragging at your keel. It is a fortune that slows you down — the kind of weight that makes you a target and steals the speed you need to escape.

The mathematics are brutal. If you are caught, you cannot outrun a frigate. Not with this load. The war chest is both the prize and the anchor.

choices.text: Keep the chest — bear the weight and the risk
Dump the chest overboard — speed is survival

s_76.text:
You give the order. The crew hesitates — fifty thousand pounds, over the side — but Pell does not hesitate. He understands.

"You heard the Captain. Get it over."

Four men heave the war chest to the gunwale. It teeters for a moment — the Crown seal catching the light — and then it goes over. The splash is enormous. The chest sinks fast, the weight of all that gold pulling it down into the deep water between the Shallows and Nassau.

Gone.

The Adamant rises immediately. You can feel her lift — the helm lightens, the bow comes up, the speed returns. She is herself again. Light. Fast. Alive.

Pell watches the bubbles where the chest went down. "Marked the position in my head, Captain. Just in case."

"Good man, Pell."

The letters. That is what matters. The gold can wait.

choices.text: Run for Nassau at speed

s_77.text:
Dawn. The eastern sky is a strip of fire above a flat sea. The Adamant sits at anchor in the lagoon, her hull reflected in the still water, every line doubled. The crew is asleep below.

You are awake. You have been awake for hours, sitting in your cabin with the brass tube in your hands, reading the letters again by candlelight.

Three letters. Three sheets of heavy paper. Ashford's handwriting — careful, measured, the hand of a man who knows that what he is writing is a lie and is determined to make it a convincing one.

The first: the cover letter to the Governor. The San Cristóbal lost to weather. The prize money disbursed. The two hundred and fourteen dead dismissed in a single clause.

The second: an inventory of the prize money. Forty-seven thousand pounds sterling, divided among Ashford's officers according to a schedule that has nothing to do with Admiralty rules. The lion's share to Ashford himself.

The third: a private note to someone unnamed. *"The matter is concluded. No witness survived in a position to contradict the account. I trust this will not be revisited."*

No witness survived. He made sure of that.

You roll the letters carefully and replace them in the tube. The candle gutters. Outside, the first light is touching the lagoon.

choices.text: Begin preparations to leave the Shallows

s_78.text:
The reef at low tide.

Standing at the Adamant's bow, you can see the entire structure of the Shallows — the coral heads breaking the surface in a ragged line, the channels between them dark with depth, the foam where the Atlantic pushes through the gaps. It is a maze. A beautiful, lethal maze.

Pell points to the main channel — the one you came through. "Tide is falling, Captain. That channel will be impassable in three hours. If we are going, we go now."

The alternative channels are narrower. Shallower. More dangerous. The main channel is the safe passage, and the window is closing.

The lagoon is calm behind you. The wreck is below. Everything you came for is aboard. There is nothing left to do here except leave.

choices.text: Make for the main channel now

s_79.text:
The Adamant's repairs will take days. The hull damage from the coral — or the cannon fire, or both — has opened seams below the waterline that cannot be patched at sea. She needs a proper careening: hauled out, stripped, re-caulked, re-coppered.

But that is a problem for after you have dealt with what is inside the brass tube.

You walk through Nassau in the early morning light. The town is waking up — fishermen heading for the waterfront, women carrying water from the well, children running barefoot through the dirt streets. A normal morning. A morning that does not know what is coming.

The Harbour Council building is ahead. The Crown marines are standing at the corner of Bay Street. Ashford's letter is in your pocket.

Nassau is balanced on a knife's edge. The blockade. The Correspondence. The gold. The reckoning that has been building since you first sailed into this harbour.

What happens next will determine whether this republic survives or falls.

choices.text: Face what comes next

s_80.text:
The last look.

You stand at the Adamant's stern as the Shallows fall away behind. The reef is a dark line on the southern horizon, breaking the swell. The lagoon — that impossibly clear, impossibly beautiful trap of coral and warm water — is invisible from here. Just ocean.

Somewhere beneath that water, the San Cristóbal rests. Her captain in his cabin. Her guns pointing at the dark. Her name carved into her stern, waiting for someone to read it.

You read it. You took what she was holding. And now you are carrying it north, to a port that does not know yet what you have found.

The wind fills the sails. The Adamant moves. Nassau is ahead.

Everything is ahead.

choices.text: Sail north — to Nassau, and to the reckoning
